description:
You are assisting a network engineer who has been called in to triage sporadic packet-loss incidents on several routers.  
The engineer already collected raw traceroute diagnostics and stored them beneath:

  /home/user/network_logs/

Your job is to do a quick “timeout census” so the engineer can see at a glance where the biggest problems lie.  
Only standard GNU/Linux command-line tools available on a minimal distribution may be used (e.g. find, xargs, sed, grep, sort, awk, cut, tr, wc, etc.).

REQUIRED END-STATE
==================

1. All file names ending in “.trace” anywhere under /home/user/network_logs **must have the string “timeout” changed to upper-case “TIMEOUT” in-place** (case-sensitive replacement, leaving every other character untouched).  
   • After you are done there must be **zero** lower-case “timeout” tokens left inside any .trace file.  
   • “TIMEOUT” must appear exactly as spelled (all caps, no leading/trailing spaces altered).

2. Immediately next to every .trace file you must create a companion file whose name is the original file name plus “.count”.  
   • Each .count file must contain **only one line** with **only one integer**: the number of “TIMEOUT” occurrences that were found in its associated .trace file **after** the conversion.  
   • The file must end with a single trailing newline (Unix “\n”).  
   • Example path: /home/user/network_logs/region1/router1.trace.count

3. Produce a consolidated CSV summary called:

   /home/user/network_audit/connectivity_report.log

   • One line per .trace file, in **alphabetical order of the path relative to /home/user/network_logs**.  
   • Format of each line:  
     <relative_path_to_trace_file>,<TIMEOUT_COUNT>  
     (No spaces before or after the comma.)  
   • After the last router entry add a final line with the grand total of timeouts across all traces:  
     TOTAL_TIMEOUTS,<SUM>  
   • The file must use Unix line endings and **no extra blank lines** anywhere.

   Example of the exact required shape (the concrete numbers will depend on what you find):

     region1/router1.trace,3
     region1/router2.trace,0
     region2/router3.trace,2
     region2/backup/router4.trace,5
     TOTAL_TIMEOUTS,10

4. All directories you create must be world-readable (mode 755) and all new files world-readable (mode 644) so that the automated grader can inspect them.

CONSTRAINTS & HINTS
===================

• You are not allowed to hard-code the file names; discover them using find.  
• Use xargs (or similar batching) so you do not spawn a separate process for every single file change.  
• The whole task can be completed with roughly 5–10 well-structured shell commands; do **not** write a script file—issue the commands interactively.  
• Assume you start in an empty shell inside /home/user.  
• Make sure you create the destination directory /home/user/network_audit before writing the summary file.  
• Paths written to connectivity_report.log must be **relative** to /home/user/network_logs, not absolute.

VERIFICATION
============

The automated tests will confirm ALL of the following:

1. Every .trace file contains at least one upper-case “TIMEOUT”, and zero lower-case “timeout”.  
2. For each .trace there is exactly one sibling .count file whose single integer matches grep -c TIMEOUT on that trace.  
3. /home/user/network_audit/connectivity_report.log exists, is world-readable, strictly follows the CSV format described, has rows sorted alphabetically, and the TOTAL_TIMEOUTS line equals the arithmetic sum of the individual counts.  
4. File and directory permissions match the modes stated above.

No additional output or artifacts are expected.
truth:
Initial on-disk fixture the grader will lay down **before** the agent begins:

Directory tree:
/home/user/network_logs/
├── region1
│   ├── router1.trace
│   └── router2.trace
└── region2
    ├── router3.trace
    └── backup
        └── router4.trace

Contents (with Unix line endings):

/home/user/network_logs/region1/router1.trace
PING 10.0.0.1
success
timeout
timeout
timeout
end

/home/user/network_logs/region1/router2.trace
PING 10.0.0.2
success
end

/home/user/network_logs/region2/router3.trace
TRACE 10.1.0.1
timeout
ok
timeout
end

/home/user/network_logs/region2/backup/router4.trace
PROBE 10.2.0.1
timeout
timeout
timeout
timeout
timeout
done

Expected outcome after the agent finishes:

1. The four .trace files must look like the originals but every “timeout” is now “TIMEOUT”.  
2. Corresponding .count files with single-line integers:
   /home/user/network_logs/region1/router1.trace.count  ->  3\n
   /home/user/network_logs/region1/router2.trace.count  ->  0\n
   /home/user/network_logs/region2/router3.trace.count  ->  2\n
   /home/user/network_logs/region2/backup/router4.trace.count  ->  5\n

3. /home/user/network_audit/connectivity_report.log  exact contents:
region1/router1.trace,3
region1/router2.trace,0
region2/router3.trace,2
region2/backup/router4.trace,5
TOTAL_TIMEOUTS,10

(Each line terminated by a single LF.)

4. Directory & file modes:
   • Every directory in the created network_audit path: 755  
   • Every .count file and connectivity_report.log: 644
